## [00:00 - 03:00]

Are you ready to write some emails? I hope that over the last few lessons you have gotten a much more clear picture of how email can totally transform the way that you talk to your subscribers, the way that you interact with your community, and the way that you grow your business. Listen, words matter. The words that we use shape the way people see us, the way people feel about us, the way people trust us and like us. The words we use start to give shape to our business and ultimately will change our business. So I hope you will not take your words lightly. Now let's talk about a few things, three things actually about your email that I want you to keep in mind as you start to write all these messages. First is consistency. Consistency is the most important aspect of email. The last thing you want to do is write a ton of emails and then be completely radio silent. You want people to start to be able to rely on you, to start to see that, hey, she's here again. You want people to see that you are showing up for them. It's hard. It's almost impossible to ask those people to show up for us if they're not seeing us show up for them. And we do that with consistency. I love to talk about Oprah when I think about consistency because when I was in high school and going through all the drama of becoming a teenager and boyfriend breaking up with me, best friend hating me, I could come home and every day at 4pm, no matter what, Oprah was on. Oprah was there for me. Oprah was doing a counseling session, a life coach moment. She had some kind of sob story. There was a place for me to interact with Oprah and get a little bit of consolation to help me through my high school years. Now, sometimes there were reruns. Sometimes she was, you know, some were off on vacation, but she was still there on the TV. And I want you to think about the fact that you are Oprah to somebody. You are that consolation. You are that comfort. You are the distraction. And so you don't have to show up. You don't want to show up every single day, like Oprah did on TV, but you want to show up consistently. You want to be there in a way that they can count on you. And so consistency is number one. If you can send just one email a month, then always send one email a month. If you can send one email every two weeks, then do that. If you can send one email a week, so much better. In fact, if I were to give you my minimum of sending emails, I would say at

## [03:00 - 06:00]

least one a week. But whatever you do, keep it consistent. That brings us to the next thing I want you to remember when it comes to writing emails, and that is frequency. Now, we're not going to worry about frequency until we get consistency, right? It's like going to the gym. You know, it's not how many reps I do. It's like, will I be back tomorrow? I needed to show up at the gym for a few weeks before we figure out if I'm going to up my weights. So frequency, we're not even going to think about that until you're consistent with sending emails. But once you've gotten consistent sending one a month, one every two weeks, maybe even one a week, then you can start to level out your frequency and think about how often your audience should hear from you. Now, I like to say the minimum is one a week. You can up it to twice a week. You do not need to be in their inbox every single day unless that is the entire nature of your emails. In fact, there are entire subscription lists where this particular writer sends a note each and every morning. I wouldn't encourage you to sign up for that just yet. Let's just get to one email a week first. But we do want to be consistent, and then we do want to be as frequent as possible and as frequent as our audience can handle. If my audience wanted and showed with email opens and click through that they wanted an email from me every day, I would 100% send it. So if you can see that your audience wants more messages by seeing how many times they open your email, how many times they click through, then I would send more emails. And I do it until I start to see a drop. When I start to see that my audience, that my subscribers are either unsubscribing or starting to open less, then I back off the frequency. But hear me out. Email is the most powerful way to communicate with your followers, with your community. So if they're opening those emails, keep sending them. So we've got consistency, we've got frequency as frequent as possible. Then finally, let's talk about variety. Now, in this course, I've taught you four different ways to message your people. You're going to entertain them with stories so they like and trust you. You're going to educate them so that they understand you. You're going to showcase to them so that they need you. And then you're going to invite to them so that they want you. So how does this work out in terms of percentages? Well, I like to break down my emails in the same way that I break down my social media posts and all the other things that we present online. So in general, I like to have around 60% of my messages be entertaining. I want most people to expect that when they open that email, there's going to be something fun in there. There's going to be a story in there. They're going to get a little moment of reprieve from everyday life with the story hiding inside that email. So around 50 to 60% should be entertaining. Then

## [06:00 - 09:03]

another 20% or so should be educational. If you're a highly educational company and you see that your audience loves the educational emails, then you could break that back and pull away some of your entertaining and stories and add in more educational messages instead. Then about 20% of my emails are showcase 15 to 20%. This is where we're showing off. This is where we're giving the before and after in the transformation. I don't go heavy on those showcase emails, but I do want to pepper them in and show them as I'm getting closer to the invitation. Finally, the invite emails make up the smallest part. So they're going to be maybe five at the most 10% of the emails that I send. Now, remember, when we talked about the anatomy of an email, the CTA, the call to action, is always there. But I'm talking about with invitational emails, the ones that are strictly all about inviting, where the whole purpose of the email is to get them to click, not just to read a story, not just to learn a simple thing, but to go ahead, click and make the decision. So those are about five to 10% of my total emails. I think about the journey every time I email. So I think about the fact that at the beginning of a promotion, the beginning of a new event, I'm going to send lots of entertaining emails. Then as we get closer to an event, the things Dean's going to teach you in launching, then we're going to teach a little bit more. As we get closer to that event, then we're going to showcase a little more. And then when we get right up on the event, that's when we go heavier on the invitational emails.
